title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a marine biologist called Bella Thorne talking about the potential for mining in the deep ocean. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a marine biologist called Bella Thorne talking about the potential for mining in the deep ocean. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Bella Thorne discusses the conflict between the need for rare minerals and the preservation of deep-sea ecosystems.
extracts.audio_script:
Good morning. I’m Bella. Four thousand meters below the surface of the ocean, there is a world that we know less about than the surface of Mars. But this hidden landscape is currently the focus of a global gold rush. I’m a marine biologist. My interest in the abyss didn't start with a documentary; it actually began when I saw a [7]glowing jellyfish[/7] during a night dive off the coast of Hawaii. The idea that life could thrive in total darkness, using only chemical energy, changed how I saw the world.

The deep-sea floor is covered in 'nodules'—potato-sized rocks that are rich in cobalt, nickel, and copper. These are essential for the batteries in our electric cars. I’ve found that the [13]environmental footprint[/13] of land-based mining is so high that many companies are looking to the ocean as a cleaner alternative. However, we don't yet understand the consequences of disturbing the seabed.

The biggest technical challenge is the 'sediment plume'. When we mine the floor, we kick up massive clouds of dust. I noticed that [8]clogged gills[/8] are a major risk for the deep-sea creatures that live in these areas. Many of these animals are filter-feeders; they survive by straining tiny particles of food from the water. If the water is full of mud, they simply can't breathe or eat. It could wipe out entire ecosystems before we’ve even named the species that live there.

We are also worried about noise pollution. The deep ocean is incredibly quiet. I’ve found that [14]sonar interference[/14] from mining machinery could disrupt the communication of whales and other deep-diving mammals. They rely on sound to navigate and find food. If we fill the ocean with the roar of heavy machinery, we are effectively blinding them.

Technology is helping us monitor these risks. We use 'autonomous gliders' that can stay underwater for months at a time. I noticed that [9]oxygen levels[/9] are the most important indicator of a healthy deep-sea site. If the mining process causes these levels to drop, it’s a sign that the bacterial balance of the ocean is being destroyed. These gliders give us a real-time warning system.

There is also a question of ownership. Who owns the bottom of the ocean? I’ve found that the [10]legal framework[/10] for international waters is still being written. We need a system that ensures the benefits of these resources are shared fairly, rather than just going to the wealthiest nations. It’s a massive diplomatic challenge.

Looking forward, I believe we should focus on 'circular economics' rather than finding new places to mine. I’m worried that the [11]battery recycling[/11] industry is not growing fast enough. If we could recover the minerals from our old phones and laptops more efficiently, we might not need to touch the deep ocean at all. We are currently throwing away a fortune in rare metals every year.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [12]policy brief[/12] for an upcoming UN conference on ocean health. I want to advocate for a 'moratorium'—a temporary stop—on all deep-sea mining until we have more scientific data. We only get one chance to protect this world. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: bella

questions.question: Bella’s interest in the deep sea was sparked by an encounter with a (7) during a dive.
questions.explanation: She mentions it 'began when I saw a glowing jellyfish during a night dive'.

questions.question: Bella notes that (8) could lead to the death of many species if mining dust is not controlled.
questions.explanation: She noticed that 'clogged gills are a major risk for the deep-sea creatures'.

questions.question: Bella explains that monitoring (9) is the best way to check for environmental damage.
questions.explanation: She noticed that 'oxygen levels are the most important indicator of a healthy deep-sea site'.

questions.question: The lack of an established (10) makes it difficult to decide who can profit from deep-sea resources.
questions.explanation: She's found that 'the legal framework for international waters is still being written'.

questions.question: Bella is currently drafting a (12) to present to the United Nations.

questions.question: Companies are interested in the ocean because the (13) of mining on land is too great.

questions.question: Bella states that (14) is a serious concern for the safety of marine mammals.
